sections.heading: Backflow Preventer Installation Compliance Guide
sections.body:
The installation and testing of backflow preventer devices are essential for ensuring the safety of the water supply and compliance with state and local regulations. Hiring a certified backflow professionals to perform backflow prevention assembly installation and testing is key to ensuring compliance with state and municipal water department regulations.

As AWWA Certified backflow testers, cross connection control experts and commercial backflow specialists, we provide leading southern california commercial clients managed turn-key backflow installation, testing and repair services to ensure compliance and and avoid costly fines and civil liabilities.

For a more detailed review of backflow prevention device installation and the importance of backflow preventer installation by licenced & certified backflow professionals please reference our
Backflow Device Installation Frequently Asked Questions

tabs.label: Backflow Installation Requirements
tabs.title: Local Municipality Backflow Installation Requirements
tabs.body:
The first step in any backflow preventer device installation is determining the specific requirements for your property, system, and jurisdiction.

This involves a careful review of local plumbing codes, water district regulations, and any additional city or county ordinances that govern backflow prevention. Requirements can vary significantly depending on the type of water system you’re connecting to—whether it’s domestic potable water, irrigation, fire protection, or industrial processes.

A hazard level assessment must also be conducted to classify the property’s water system as low, moderate, or high hazard. This classification determines the type of backflow preventer device that must be installed.

For example, systems that handle chemicals, pesticides, or bodily fluids (such as those in medical or laboratory environments) are classified as high hazard and require more robust protection like a Reduced Pressure Zone Assembly (RPZ).

These and other factors must be considered before selecting the correct backflow preventer device and beginning installation.

Moreover, some backflow installation scenarios may require permitting. That said, not all municipalities explicitly require an backflow installation permit in all cases even though in many instances a separate permit for the installation of backflow preventer devices is indeed required.

If, for example, the installation involves work within the public right-of-way, such as connecting to the city water main or meter box, a separate permit from the Public Works Department is required.

Likewise, for existing buildings, the installation of a backflow preventer device may be required depending on the extent of remodeling and the potential for contamination to the public water supply.

If the installation is part of a larger plumbing project or involves modifications to the existing plumbing system, for instance, a plumbing permit or water quality permit may be necessary.

The process includes submitting plans for review and obtaining approval before installation.

Before commencing any installation, it's advisable to contact the particular municipality's Public Works or Building and Safety Division to determine the specific permits needed for your project.

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabs.label: Selecting Approved Backflow Prevention Devices
tabs.title: Selecting Municipality Approved Backflow Preventer Devices
tabs.body:
Once regulatory and site-specific requirements are understood, the next step is selecting the appropriate backflow prevention assembly.

The device must match the assessed hazard level of the system and be listed on an approved devices list, commonly issued by health departments or testing institutions like the University of Southern California's Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research (USC-FCCCHR).

Each municipality or water district has its own approved device list, installation standards conforming to standards established by the American Water Works Association (AWWA) and the Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research at the University of Southern California.

For high-hazard applications, such as those involving chemical use or systems with the potential for significant contamination, an RPZ assembly is typically required. RPZs provide the highest level of protection and include a relief valve to discharge any backflow.

For medium-hazard systems, such as standard irrigation without chemical injection, a Double Check Valve Assembly (DCVA) is often sufficient.

Systems at lower risk, like residential irrigation or hose bibs, may be protected using Pressure Vacuum Breakers (PVBs) or Atmospheric Vacuum Breakers (AVBs), though the latter cannot be subjected to continuous pressure.

Selecting the wrong device can result in a failed inspection or, worse, contamination of the potable water supply.

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabs.label: Reduced Pressure Principle Backflow Device Installation
tabs.title: Reduced Pressure Principle Backflow Assembly Installation
tabs.body: In California, the installation of a Reduced Pressure Principle Backflow Preventer (RP) is governed by both state regulations and local water agency requirements. These devices are essential for protecting potable water supplies from contamination due to backflow caused by backpressure or backsiphonage. Typical Applications of Reduced Pressure Principle Devices Reduced Pressure Principle backflow preventers are the most common type of backflow preventer in California and can be used for a wide variety of applications. Commercial buildings, and some homes will almost exclusively require this type of device. Here are some examples of potential installations: Main plumbing line, Irrigation line, Fire Sprinkler System, Pump System, Pool, Food Processing Line, Chiller, Multi Family Home, Brewery, Hospital, Dialysis Machine, Reclaimed Water Supply, Auxiliary Water Supply, Autoclave, Autopsy Table, Boiler, Commercial Dishwashers, Car Washing Machine, Carbonators, Chemical Dispenser, Cookers, Heat Exchanger, Lab Equipment, Portable Cleaning Equipment, & Sewage Ejector. Statewide Installation Requirements Under the Cross Connection Control Policy Handbook, RP devices must: - Be Approved: Devices must be approved by recognized testing organizations, such as the University of Southern California's Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research (USC-FCCCHR). - Conform to Standards: RP devices must, at a minimum, conform to the AWWA Standard C506-78 (R83) for Reduced Pressure Principle Type Backflow Prevention Devices. - Be Installed Above Ground: RP devices must be installed above ground and as close as practical to the user's connection. - Be Readily Accessible: Devices should be installed in a location that is readily accessible for testing and maintenance. General Installation Guidelines While specific requirements can vary by local jurisdiction, common installation practices include: - Height Above Grade: Install the RP device with the relief valve at least 12 inches above the surrounding ground or floor level to prevent submersion and allow proper drainage. Maximum height for relief valve shall be no greater than 36". - Clearances: Maintain adequate clearance around the device for maintenance and testing, typically a minimum of 12 inches on all sides. - Drainage: Provide a drain funnel or air gap drain line to safely discharge water from the relief valve without causing flooding or contamination. - Protection from Freezing: In areas subject to freezing temperatures, protect the device with insulation or enclosures, ensuring that the relief valve discharge is not obstructed. - No Subgrade Installations: Do not install RP devices in pits or vaults below ground level, as this can lead to flooding and contamination risks. Local Considerations Local water agencies may have additional requirements: - Proximity to Water Meter: Some jurisdictions require the RP device to be installed within a specific distance (e.g., 5 to 10 feet) from the water meter. - Permits and Inspections: Installation may require permits and must be inspected by the local water authority. - Testing: Devices must be tested upon installation and annually thereafter by a certified backflow prevention assembly tester. For a comprehensive understanding of backflow prevention installation requirements, refer to the California Code of Regulations, Cross Connection Control Policy Handbook, and consult with your local water authority or Call Backflow Test Pros for guidance on backflow device installation best practices. ‍

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabs.label: Double Check Valve Backflow Preventer Installation
tabs.title: Double Check Valve Backflow Preventer Installation
tabs.body:
In California, the installation of a Double Check Valve Assembly (DCVA) for backflow prevention is governed by the California Code of Regulations (CCR), and Cross Connection Policy Handbook. These assemblies are designed to prevent non-health hazard pollutants from entering the potable water supply due to backpressure or backsiphonage.

Typical Applications for DCV- Double Check Valve Backflow Devices

Double Check Valve Backflow Preventers are typically installed on fire sprinkler systems or on hazards that pose a low level threat to the water supply, which are called pollutants.

Typical applications include Fire Systems, and Main Lines for Homes posing minimal cross connection risk.

Statewide Installation Requirements

While specific requirements can vary by local jurisdiction, common installation practices include:

- Device Approval and Standards: DCVAs must conform to the American Water Works Association (AWWA) Standard C510 for Double Check Valve Backflow Prevention Assemblies.
- Installation Location and Orientation: The assembly should be installed as close as practical to the user's connection. Above-grade installation is preferred, though DCVAs may be installed below grade in a vault when they remain readily accessible for testing and maintenance.
- Accessibility and Clearances: Maintain adequate clearance around the device, typically a minimum of 12 inches on all sides, to facilitate maintenance and testing.

General Installation Guidelines

- Orientation: DCVAs are typically installed horizontally, as per manufacturer specifications.
- Protection from Freezing: In areas subject to freezing temperatures, protect the device with insulation or enclosures, ensuring that the relief valve discharge is not obstructed.
- Proper Drainage: Ensure that the installation site has adequate drainage to prevent water accumulation, especially if installed below grade.

Local Considerations

Local water agencies may have additional requirements:

- Permits and Inspections: Installation may require permits and must be inspected by the local water authority.
- Testing: Devices must be tested upon installation and annually thereafter by an AWWA certified backflow prevention assembly tester.

For a comprehensive understanding of backflow prevention installation requirements, refer to the California Code of Regulations, Cross Connection Control Policy Handbook, and consult with your local water authority or Call Backflow Test Pros for guidance on backflow device installation best practices.

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabs.label: Backflow Installation Site Preparation
tabs.title: Backflow Preventer Device Installation Site Preparation
tabs.body:
Proper site preparation is critical for a safe, compliant, and maintainable installation.

The location of the backflow preventer device must allow for required clearance and accessibility for annual testing, maintenance, and possible repairs.

Generally, at least 12 inches of clearance around the device is recommended, and the assembly must be installed at a minimum elevation, often 12 inches above grade or above the highest downstream outlet, depending on the device type.

RPZ assemblies, which have a relief valve that can discharge water under pressure, must be installed in locations that can accommodate drainage, such as above a floor drain or with a dedicated indirect waste line. When installing outdoors, the device should be protected from freezing temperatures using an insulated and ventilated enclosure or installed indoors if allowed by code.

Additionally, installers should ensure a solid and level mounting surface such as a concrete pad or wall bracket to support the device and prevent shifting or vibrations that could damage the assembly or connected piping.

In some cases, particularly for larger commercial systems, isolation valves and drain lines may also need to be pre-installed or planned for in advance.

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabs.label: Backflow Installation Process
tabs.title: Backflow Preventer Assembly Installation Process
tabs.body: After site preparation, the actual installation of the backflow prevention assembly can proceed. The water supply must first be shut off upstream to prevent flooding or contamination during installation. Shutoff valves are installed both upstream and downstream of the assembly to allow for isolation during future testing or maintenance. Many jurisdictions also require test cocks to be installed on the device to facilitate testing. The orientation of the device is crucial; some devices such as RPZs and DCVAs must be installed in a horizontal configuration unless otherwise rated by the manufacturer for vertical use. PVBs and AVBs must be installed vertically and always above the highest downstream outlet to function correctly. The choice of pipe materials is also important—copper, brass, stainless steel, and approved plastic materials such as Schedule 40 or 80 PVC may be used, but galvanized steel should be avoided in potable systems due to corrosion concerns. All connections should be secure and allow for easy disassembly, usually by using unions or flanges. Once the device is installed and secured in place, it should be labeled appropriately, and the site should be cleaned up and inspected for leaks or alignment issues. ‍

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabs.label: Initial Backflow Testing and Certification
tabs.title: Initial Backflow Testing and Certification Following Installation
tabs.body: The final stage of installation involves testing and certifying the backflow preventer test to ensure it operates correctly and provides the level of protection required. This initial test is a critical step in verifying the integrity and safety of the water system and establishes a baseline for future annual testing and maintenance. Proper records allow property owners, municipalities, and testing professionals to track device performance over time. This must be done by a certified backflow preventer assembly tester, who will use calibrated testing equipment to evaluate the device’s check valves and relief valves under both normal and fault conditions. The initial test must be completed immediately after installation, and the results must typically be submitted to the local water authority or environmental health department using a standardized form. The tester checks key components such as check valves and relief valves for proper operation, and records pressure differentials to confirm the device meets specified performance criteria. After performing a test, certified testers must submit a backflow test report to the local water authority. This report must include: - Test results (e.g., pressure readings, flow rates, and whether the device passed or failed). - Testers’ certifications and contact information.Device information, including manufacturer, model number, and serial number. - Description of any repairs if the device failed the test and was subsequently fixed or replaced. Most municipalities require the test report to be submitted within a few days or weeks of the test, often by the end of the calendar year for annual tests. Property owners are responsible for keeping a record of all backflow test results and ensuring timely compliance with future testing schedules. ‍

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabs.label: Installation Documentation and Record Keeping
tabs.title: Backflow Preventer Installation Initial Test Report Documentation
tabs.body: Following the installation of a backflow preventer device, all findings are documented in a test report, which is often submitted to the appropriate local or municipal authority. Backflow test report documentation is a critical part of the backflow testing process, as it serves as an official record of the test results, confirming that backflow preventer devices are functioning properly. Proper documentation protects the tester, property owner, and any contractors involved in the installation and maintenance of the backflow preventer. These reports are usually submitted to local authorities, water utilities, or building owners to ensure compliance with local regulations and water safety standards. The reporting requirements for a backflow prevention device test can vary depending on local and regional regulations. In most cases, the test results are submitted to the city’s water authority or municipal utility department responsible for drinking water quality and infrastructure. In some areas, especially where there is no municipal water department, the county health department or county water authority may oversee backflow prevention programs. Factors That Determine Who Gets the Report: - Jurisdictional Authority: The responsible agency is typically based on who manages the local water supply—city or county. - Type of Property: Commercial, industrial, or agricultural properties may be subject to additional oversight, possibly involving the county health department. Residential installations are usually handled by the city or local water purveyor. - Type of Device and Risk Level: High-hazard applications (e.g., chemical plants, irrigation systems with fertilizers) may require reporting to multiple agencies, including environmental or health departments. Low-hazard devices may only require filing with the local water authority. - Local Ordinances: Some cities or counties have specific rules requiring submission to both city and county agencies or to a centralized database. - Best Practice: Always best to check with the local water provider or building department at the time of installation to determine exactly where test reports must be submitted. Certified testers often know the local requirements and submit reports accordingly. ‍

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabs.label: Follow Up Annual Backflow Device Testing
tabs.title: California Municipal Water Authority Backflow Testing Requirements
tabs.body: State regulations mandate and California municipalities generally require annual testing of backflow prevention devices for properties that are connected to the public water supply and pose a potential contamination risk. This includes commercial properties, multi-family residential buildings, industrial sites, and certain high-risk areas. Only certified backflow testers are authorized to conduct backflow testing in California. Testers must be certified by a recognized agency, such as the American Backflow Prevention Association (ABPA) or other approved certifying organizations. Backflow test reports are submitted to the municipal office responsible for overseeing backflow compliance either through the document submission electronic portal, by email or by the postal service as specified by each city. In many instances, test reports may also be required to be submitted to the County Health Department and the local water supplier. Reports should be submitted individually by location on approved forms as PDF attachments. ‍

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation

sections.heading: Municipal Water Authority Backflow Installation Regulations
sections.body:
Compliance with local backflow prevention regulations is governed by a combination of California State Water Resources Control Board guidelines, plumbing codes, Department of Public Health (CDPH) guidelines, and the specific ordinances and requirements set by local municipalities and water districts.

Beginning July 1, 2025, CCCPH will be the primary standard enforced statewide reflecting the regulatory shift from Title 17 of the California Code of Regulations to the new Cross Connection Control Policy Handbook (CCCPH). CCCPH aims to standardize how local water purveyors implement cross-connection control, improving public safety. Agencies will now have clearer authority to mandate testing, repairs, and shutdowns for non-compliant properties.

Under the new regulatory guidelines, American Water Works Association (AWWA) Certification is required and is the only agency recognized under new laws for backflow testing certification.

County Health Department and Municipality Backflow Testing Requirements:

With Backflow Test Pros' Managed Backflow Installation, Testing and Maintenance service, there is no need to spend hours going over local water utility ordinances, requirements, deadlines, and fees, guidelines, reporting schedules and forms. Everything is Done for You to simply the backflow installation, testing and maintenance process and ensure compliance with local regulations.

tabGroups.label: Backflow Installation Requirements
tabGroups.title: Local Municipality Backflow Installation Requirements
tabGroups.body:
The first step in any backflow preventer device installation is determining the specific requirements for your property, system, and jurisdiction.

This involves a careful review of local plumbing codes, water district regulations, and any additional city or county ordinances that govern backflow prevention. Requirements can vary significantly depending on the type of water system you’re connecting to—whether it’s domestic potable water, irrigation, fire protection, or industrial processes.

A hazard level assessment must also be conducted to classify the property’s water system as low, moderate, or high hazard. This classification determines the type of backflow preventer device that must be installed.

For example, systems that handle chemicals, pesticides, or bodily fluids (such as those in medical or laboratory environments) are classified as high hazard and require more robust protection like a Reduced Pressure Zone Assembly (RPZ).

These and other factors must be considered before selecting the correct backflow preventer device and beginning installation.

Moreover, some backflow installation scenarios may require permitting. That said, not all municipalities explicitly require an backflow installation permit in all cases even though in many instances a separate permit for the installation of backflow preventer devices is indeed required.

If, for example, the installation involves work within the public right-of-way, such as connecting to the city water main or meter box, a separate permit from the Public Works Department is required.

Likewise, for existing buildings, the installation of a backflow preventer device may be required depending on the extent of remodeling and the potential for contamination to the public water supply.

If the installation is part of a larger plumbing project or involves modifications to the existing plumbing system, for instance, a plumbing permit or water quality permit may be necessary.

The process includes submitting plans for review and obtaining approval before installation.

Before commencing any installation, it's advisable to contact the particular municipality's Public Works or Building and Safety Division to determine the specific permits needed for your project.

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabGroups.label: Selecting Approved Backflow Prevention Devices
tabGroups.title: Selecting Municipality Approved Backflow Preventer Devices
tabGroups.body:
Once regulatory and site-specific requirements are understood, the next step is selecting the appropriate backflow prevention assembly.

The device must match the assessed hazard level of the system and be listed on an approved devices list, commonly issued by health departments or testing institutions like the University of Southern California's Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research (USC-FCCCHR).

Each municipality or water district has its own approved device list, installation standards conforming to standards established by the American Water Works Association (AWWA) and the Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research at the University of Southern California.

For high-hazard applications, such as those involving chemical use or systems with the potential for significant contamination, an RPZ assembly is typically required. RPZs provide the highest level of protection and include a relief valve to discharge any backflow.

For medium-hazard systems, such as standard irrigation without chemical injection, a Double Check Valve Assembly (DCVA) is often sufficient.

Systems at lower risk, like residential irrigation or hose bibs, may be protected using Pressure Vacuum Breakers (PVBs) or Atmospheric Vacuum Breakers (AVBs), though the latter cannot be subjected to continuous pressure.

Selecting the wrong device can result in a failed inspection or, worse, contamination of the potable water supply.

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabGroups.label: Reduced Pressure Principle Backflow Device Installation
tabGroups.title: Reduced Pressure Principle Backflow Assembly Installation
tabGroups.body: In California, the installation of a Reduced Pressure Principle Backflow Preventer (RP) is governed by both state regulations and local water agency requirements. These devices are essential for protecting potable water supplies from contamination due to backflow caused by backpressure or backsiphonage. Typical Applications of Reduced Pressure Principle Devices Reduced Pressure Principle backflow preventers are the most common type of backflow preventer in California and can be used for a wide variety of applications. Commercial buildings, and some homes will almost exclusively require this type of device. Here are some examples of potential installations: Main plumbing line, Irrigation line, Fire Sprinkler System, Pump System, Pool, Food Processing Line, Chiller, Multi Family Home, Brewery, Hospital, Dialysis Machine, Reclaimed Water Supply, Auxiliary Water Supply, Autoclave, Autopsy Table, Boiler, Commercial Dishwashers, Car Washing Machine, Carbonators, Chemical Dispenser, Cookers, Heat Exchanger, Lab Equipment, Portable Cleaning Equipment, & Sewage Ejector. Statewide Installation Requirements Under the Cross Connection Control Policy Handbook, RP devices must: - Be Approved: Devices must be approved by recognized testing organizations, such as the University of Southern California's Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research (USC-FCCCHR). - Conform to Standards: RP devices must, at a minimum, conform to the AWWA Standard C506-78 (R83) for Reduced Pressure Principle Type Backflow Prevention Devices. - Be Installed Above Ground: RP devices must be installed above ground and as close as practical to the user's connection. - Be Readily Accessible: Devices should be installed in a location that is readily accessible for testing and maintenance. General Installation Guidelines While specific requirements can vary by local jurisdiction, common installation practices include: - Height Above Grade: Install the RP device with the relief valve at least 12 inches above the surrounding ground or floor level to prevent submersion and allow proper drainage. Maximum height for relief valve shall be no greater than 36". - Clearances: Maintain adequate clearance around the device for maintenance and testing, typically a minimum of 12 inches on all sides. - Drainage: Provide a drain funnel or air gap drain line to safely discharge water from the relief valve without causing flooding or contamination. - Protection from Freezing: In areas subject to freezing temperatures, protect the device with insulation or enclosures, ensuring that the relief valve discharge is not obstructed. - No Subgrade Installations: Do not install RP devices in pits or vaults below ground level, as this can lead to flooding and contamination risks. Local Considerations Local water agencies may have additional requirements: - Proximity to Water Meter: Some jurisdictions require the RP device to be installed within a specific distance (e.g., 5 to 10 feet) from the water meter. - Permits and Inspections: Installation may require permits and must be inspected by the local water authority. - Testing: Devices must be tested upon installation and annually thereafter by a certified backflow prevention assembly tester. For a comprehensive understanding of backflow prevention installation requirements, refer to the California Code of Regulations, Cross Connection Control Policy Handbook, and consult with your local water authority or Call Backflow Test Pros for guidance on backflow device installation best practices. ‍

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabGroups.label: Double Check Valve Backflow Preventer Installation
tabGroups.title: Double Check Valve Backflow Preventer Installation
tabGroups.body:
In California, the installation of a Double Check Valve Assembly (DCVA) for backflow prevention is governed by the California Code of Regulations (CCR), and Cross Connection Policy Handbook. These assemblies are designed to prevent non-health hazard pollutants from entering the potable water supply due to backpressure or backsiphonage.

Typical Applications for DCV- Double Check Valve Backflow Devices

Double Check Valve Backflow Preventers are typically installed on fire sprinkler systems or on hazards that pose a low level threat to the water supply, which are called pollutants.

Typical applications include Fire Systems, and Main Lines for Homes posing minimal cross connection risk.

Statewide Installation Requirements

While specific requirements can vary by local jurisdiction, common installation practices include:

- Device Approval and Standards: DCVAs must conform to the American Water Works Association (AWWA) Standard C510 for Double Check Valve Backflow Prevention Assemblies.
- Installation Location and Orientation: The assembly should be installed as close as practical to the user's connection. Above-grade installation is preferred, though DCVAs may be installed below grade in a vault when they remain readily accessible for testing and maintenance.
- Accessibility and Clearances: Maintain adequate clearance around the device, typically a minimum of 12 inches on all sides, to facilitate maintenance and testing.

General Installation Guidelines

- Orientation: DCVAs are typically installed horizontally, as per manufacturer specifications.
- Protection from Freezing: In areas subject to freezing temperatures, protect the device with insulation or enclosures, ensuring that the relief valve discharge is not obstructed.
- Proper Drainage: Ensure that the installation site has adequate drainage to prevent water accumulation, especially if installed below grade.

Local Considerations

Local water agencies may have additional requirements:

- Permits and Inspections: Installation may require permits and must be inspected by the local water authority.
- Testing: Devices must be tested upon installation and annually thereafter by an AWWA certified backflow prevention assembly tester.

For a comprehensive understanding of backflow prevention installation requirements, refer to the California Code of Regulations, Cross Connection Control Policy Handbook, and consult with your local water authority or Call Backflow Test Pros for guidance on backflow device installation best practices.

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabGroups.label: Backflow Installation Site Preparation
tabGroups.title: Backflow Preventer Device Installation Site Preparation
tabGroups.body:
Proper site preparation is critical for a safe, compliant, and maintainable installation.

The location of the backflow preventer device must allow for required clearance and accessibility for annual testing, maintenance, and possible repairs.

Generally, at least 12 inches of clearance around the device is recommended, and the assembly must be installed at a minimum elevation, often 12 inches above grade or above the highest downstream outlet, depending on the device type.

RPZ assemblies, which have a relief valve that can discharge water under pressure, must be installed in locations that can accommodate drainage, such as above a floor drain or with a dedicated indirect waste line. When installing outdoors, the device should be protected from freezing temperatures using an insulated and ventilated enclosure or installed indoors if allowed by code.

Additionally, installers should ensure a solid and level mounting surface such as a concrete pad or wall bracket to support the device and prevent shifting or vibrations that could damage the assembly or connected piping.

In some cases, particularly for larger commercial systems, isolation valves and drain lines may also need to be pre-installed or planned for in advance.

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabGroups.label: Backflow Installation Process
tabGroups.title: Backflow Preventer Assembly Installation Process
tabGroups.body: After site preparation, the actual installation of the backflow prevention assembly can proceed. The water supply must first be shut off upstream to prevent flooding or contamination during installation. Shutoff valves are installed both upstream and downstream of the assembly to allow for isolation during future testing or maintenance. Many jurisdictions also require test cocks to be installed on the device to facilitate testing. The orientation of the device is crucial; some devices such as RPZs and DCVAs must be installed in a horizontal configuration unless otherwise rated by the manufacturer for vertical use. PVBs and AVBs must be installed vertically and always above the highest downstream outlet to function correctly. The choice of pipe materials is also important—copper, brass, stainless steel, and approved plastic materials such as Schedule 40 or 80 PVC may be used, but galvanized steel should be avoided in potable systems due to corrosion concerns. All connections should be secure and allow for easy disassembly, usually by using unions or flanges. Once the device is installed and secured in place, it should be labeled appropriately, and the site should be cleaned up and inspected for leaks or alignment issues. ‍

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabGroups.label: Initial Backflow Testing and Certification
tabGroups.title: Initial Backflow Testing and Certification Following Installation
tabGroups.body: The final stage of installation involves testing and certifying the backflow preventer test to ensure it operates correctly and provides the level of protection required. This initial test is a critical step in verifying the integrity and safety of the water system and establishes a baseline for future annual testing and maintenance. Proper records allow property owners, municipalities, and testing professionals to track device performance over time. This must be done by a certified backflow preventer assembly tester, who will use calibrated testing equipment to evaluate the device’s check valves and relief valves under both normal and fault conditions. The initial test must be completed immediately after installation, and the results must typically be submitted to the local water authority or environmental health department using a standardized form. The tester checks key components such as check valves and relief valves for proper operation, and records pressure differentials to confirm the device meets specified performance criteria. After performing a test, certified testers must submit a backflow test report to the local water authority. This report must include: - Test results (e.g., pressure readings, flow rates, and whether the device passed or failed). - Testers’ certifications and contact information.Device information, including manufacturer, model number, and serial number. - Description of any repairs if the device failed the test and was subsequently fixed or replaced. Most municipalities require the test report to be submitted within a few days or weeks of the test, often by the end of the calendar year for annual tests. Property owners are responsible for keeping a record of all backflow test results and ensuring timely compliance with future testing schedules. ‍

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabGroups.label: Installation Documentation and Record Keeping
tabGroups.title: Backflow Preventer Installation Initial Test Report Documentation
tabGroups.body: Following the installation of a backflow preventer device, all findings are documented in a test report, which is often submitted to the appropriate local or municipal authority. Backflow test report documentation is a critical part of the backflow testing process, as it serves as an official record of the test results, confirming that backflow preventer devices are functioning properly. Proper documentation protects the tester, property owner, and any contractors involved in the installation and maintenance of the backflow preventer. These reports are usually submitted to local authorities, water utilities, or building owners to ensure compliance with local regulations and water safety standards. The reporting requirements for a backflow prevention device test can vary depending on local and regional regulations. In most cases, the test results are submitted to the city’s water authority or municipal utility department responsible for drinking water quality and infrastructure. In some areas, especially where there is no municipal water department, the county health department or county water authority may oversee backflow prevention programs. Factors That Determine Who Gets the Report: - Jurisdictional Authority: The responsible agency is typically based on who manages the local water supply—city or county. - Type of Property: Commercial, industrial, or agricultural properties may be subject to additional oversight, possibly involving the county health department. Residential installations are usually handled by the city or local water purveyor. - Type of Device and Risk Level: High-hazard applications (e.g., chemical plants, irrigation systems with fertilizers) may require reporting to multiple agencies, including environmental or health departments. Low-hazard devices may only require filing with the local water authority. - Local Ordinances: Some cities or counties have specific rules requiring submission to both city and county agencies or to a centralized database. - Best Practice: Always best to check with the local water provider or building department at the time of installation to determine exactly where test reports must be submitted. Certified testers often know the local requirements and submit reports accordingly. ‍

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation!

tabGroups.label: Follow Up Annual Backflow Device Testing
tabGroups.title: California Municipal Water Authority Backflow Testing Requirements
tabGroups.body: State regulations mandate and California municipalities generally require annual testing of backflow prevention devices for properties that are connected to the public water supply and pose a potential contamination risk. This includes commercial properties, multi-family residential buildings, industrial sites, and certain high-risk areas. Only certified backflow testers are authorized to conduct backflow testing in California. Testers must be certified by a recognized agency, such as the American Backflow Prevention Association (ABPA) or other approved certifying organizations. Backflow test reports are submitted to the municipal office responsible for overseeing backflow compliance either through the document submission electronic portal, by email or by the postal service as specified by each city. In many instances, test reports may also be required to be submitted to the County Health Department and the local water supplier. Reports should be submitted individually by location on approved forms as PDF attachments. ‍

links.label: Call for Free Testing with Your Backflow Installation

sections.heading: Backflow Preventer Installation Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: Why do I need a backflow prevention device?
items.answer: Backflow prevention is vital for protecting both individual properties and the public water supply from contamination. Without it, events such as pressure drops in the municipal water supply can cause dirty water to siphon back into clean lines. For example, fertilizers or pesticides from an irrigation system could flow back into the home’s drinking water during such an event. By installing a backflow preventer, you comply with safety regulations and help protect the community from waterborne diseases and pollutants. Backflow devices are required by plumbing codes and water authorities to protect drinking water from contamination due to backpressure or backsiphonage. It helps keep your water supply safe. ‍

items.question: Do I need a permit to install a backflow preventer?
items.answer: Yes, in most cases a permit is required before installing a backflow device, especially for commercial systems or when connecting to municipal water. The process involves submitting a permit application along with detailed plans for review. The permit process helps ensure the device is appropriate for the hazard level and that it’s installed correctly by a licensed professional. Your plumber or backflow technician can usually handle the permitting process on your behalf, but it’s important to confirm with your local building or water department. In most cases, yes. Always check with your local building or plumbing authority. ‍

items.question: Where am I Required to Install a Backflow Prevention Device?
items.answer: Installation of approved backflow preventer devices at locations where there is a potential risk of contamination. These devices must be installed at or near the property line or immediately outside the building being served, before the first branch line leading off of the service line. The determination of the appropriate device is made after an inspection by a Cross-Connection Control Specialist. ‍

items.question: Under what Circumstances am I Required to Install a Backflow Prevention Device?
items.answer: State, county and local municipal regulations require the installation of approved backflow preventer assemblies (BPAs) to protect the water supply. Backflow preventer assemblies are required in various scenarios, including: - Irrigation Systems: To prevent contaminants from entering the potable water supply through irrigation lines. ‍ - Commercial and Industrial Properties: Facilities with potential cross-connections, such as those involving chemicals or pressurized systems, are required to install appropriate backflow preventer assemblies. - Fire Protection Systems: To prevent stagnant water from fire suppression systems from flowing back into the drinking water supply. ‍ - Auxiliary Water Sources: Properties with alternative water sources like wells or cisterns must install backflow preventer devices to prevent cross-contamination. ‍

items.question: Where is the backflow preventer installed?
items.answer: The location of the backflow preventer depends on the type of system and local regulations, but it is commonly installed outdoors near the main water meter or where the irrigation system connects to the potable water line. It should be placed in an accessible area to allow for regular inspection, maintenance, and testing. Some municipalities also require that it be installed above ground and in a weatherproof enclosure to protect it from freezing or tampering. Usually, it is installed: - Outside the building near the water meter. ‍ - Above ground for accessibility and to meet code - On irrigation systems between the main supply and sprinkler lines.

items.question: Are there differnt type of Backflow Prevention Devices?
items.answer: Absolutely. The type of backflow prevention device you need depends on the level of risk for contamination in your system. High-risk situations (such as chemical systems, commercial settings, or industrial equipment) require more sophisticated devices like RPZs, while lower-risk situations (like residential irrigation systems) may only need PVBs or DCVs. Regular testing and maintenance are essential to ensure these devices function properly and continue to protect your water supply. ‍

items.question: What types of backflow preventers are there?
items.answer: There are several types of backflow preventers, each designed for specific applications and hazard levels. An Atmospheric Vacuum Breaker (AVB) is simple and cost-effective, typically used for lawn irrigation. Pressure Vacuum Breakers (PVBs) are more durable and allow for testing. Double Check Valve Assemblies (DCVAs) are suitable for low to moderate hazard scenarios and offer better protection. Reduced Pressure Zone (RPZ) assemblies offer the highest level of protection and are required for high-hazard systems. Selecting the correct type is critical to ensure both compliance and safety. Common backflow assemblies installed include: ‍ - Atmospheric Vacuum Breaker (AVB) - Pressure Vacuum Breaker (PVB) - Double Check Valve Assembly (DCVA) - Reduced Pressure Zone Assembly (RPZ) Each type is suited for different levels of hazard and system pressures. ‍

items.question: Can I install a backflow preventer indoors?
items.answer: Backflow devices can be installed indoors in certain cases, such as in commercial boiler rooms or where outdoor installation isn’t feasible. However, the area must have adequate drainage, accessibility, and protection from leaks or water damage. Indoor installations must comply with building codes and often require additional safety features like catch basins or alarms. An experienced installer can evaluate whether your setup is appropriate for indoor installation. ‍

items.question: How much does it cost to install a backflow device?
items.answer: The cost to install a backflow preventer device varies widely based on several factors such as the type of device required, labor rates in your area, and whether the plumbing system needs any modifications. On average, residential installations range from $200 to $500, while commercial or complex systems can exceed $1,000. Additional costs may include permits, inspections, and the annual testing required by most jurisdictions. ‍ Costs vary by: - Type of device - Labor rates - Local regulations Typical range: $200 to $1,000+ (including parts and labor)

items.question: How long does installation take?
items.answer: Installation typically takes between 1 to 3 hours, depending on the type of backflow device, the location of the plumbing, and the condition of the existing system. Simple installations on residential irrigation systems are relatively quick, while more complex commercial setups or systems requiring trenching, cutting, or additional plumbing work may take longer. Your installer will be able to provide a more accurate timeline after assessing the site. ‍ Typically 1–3 hours, depending on the type of system and location. Complex systems might take longer. ‍

items.question: Is installation of a backflow device required by law?
items.answer: Yes, in many jurisdictions. Local plumbing codes or water authorities often mandate them for irrigation systems, commercial properties, and multi-unit buildings. Contact your local water utility or municipality for specifics. In most municipalities, the installation of a backflow prevention device is a legal requirement, particularly for commercial properties, multi-family residences, and homes with irrigation or fire protection systems. These regulations are often enforced by local plumbing codes or water utilities to ensure public health and safety. Failure to comply can result in fines, citations, or even disconnection of water service. Always check with your local authority to ensure compliance with regional laws. ‍

items.question: What are my backflow Device installation Responsibilities?
items.answer: Property owners are responsible for installing backflow prevention assemblies as required by the city's regulations or county health board Installations must adhere to the state, county, and municipal standards, including specific device types and installation procedures. Failure to install the required backflow preventer devices in compliance with regulations and requirements may result in the discontinuation of water service until compliance is achieved. To ensure the continued effectiveness of backflow preventer assemblies, the following backflow testing and maintenance protocols are also required: - Certified Backflow Testers: Installations and tests must be conducted by individuals certified by recognized organizations, such as the American Water Works Association (AWWA) California-Nevada Section or the County Health Care Agency. - Approved Backflow Devices: Only backflow preventer assemblies listed by recognized testing agencies are permitted. The USC-FCCCHR provides a comprehensive list of approved devices.Failure to conduct required backflow testing for properties may lead to water service termination until the device is tested and found compliant. - Post-Installation or Repair Backflow Testing: Any new installation, relocation, or repair of a backflow device requires immediate testing to confirm correct operation. - Annual Backflow Testing: Backflow preventer assemblies must be tested at least annually to ensure proper operation. - Backflow Test Record Keeping: Test results must be submitted to the appropriate regulatory agency to maintain compliance records. ‍

items.question: Who can install a backflow prevention Device?
items.answer: Backflow preventers must be installed by a licensed plumber or certified backflow technician to ensure they meet regulatory standards and function correctly. These professionals are trained to assess the risk level of your system, select the appropriate device, and install it according to code. DIY installation is strongly discouraged, as incorrect installation could cause leaks, device failure, or even violate local plumbing laws, which could lead to fines or service disruptions. ‍

items.question: What are most common standards governing backflow preventer device installations?
items.answer: - California State Water Resources Control Board - Uniform Plumbing Code (UPC) - Local municipal codes and regulations enforced by city or county water departments. - American Water Works Association (AWWA) - University of Southern California’s Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research ‍

items.question: What happens if I don’t install a required backflow device?
items.answer: Failing to install a required backflow preventer can have serious consequences. Water utilities often conduct inspections, and if your property is found to be non-compliant, you may face fines, citations, or water service disconnection until the issue is resolved. More importantly, without a backflow preventer, your property could contaminate the public water supply, posing a significant health risk and exposing you to potential legal liability. Possible consequences: - Fines or legal penalties - Water service shutoff - Risk of contaminating public water supply - Civil liability

sections.heading: AWWA Certified Backflow Testers Cross Connection Control Specialists
sections.body: Under the regulatory guidelines of the Cross Connection Control Policy Handbook (CCCPH) which replaces Title 17 of the California Code of Regulations, the American Water Works Association (AWWA) is the only agency recognized under new laws for backflow testing certification. Backflow Test Pros is AWWA Certified Backflow Tester and Approved Cross-Connect Specialist + Certified Backflow Testers with County Health Departments across Southern California. As state licensed contractors, and AWWA cross connection specialists, our team of experienced backflow experts work with city water departments throughout Southern California to protect our water, and prevent backflow contamination.

sections.heading: Two Year Warranty & Backflow Testing Included with Your Backflow Preventer Installation
sections.body: Our highly experienced backflow installationn technicians know how to conduct a hazard level assessment to determine the type of backflow preventer device that must be installed. Additionally, our certified backflow installation experts confirm whether the backflow prevention device can be placed indoors or outdoors, what kind of freeze protection or drainage is needed, and whether elevation or enclosure requirements apply before selecting the correct device and beginning installation. ‍ Relying on Backflow Testing Pros' licensed, certified and bonded professionals ensures that your backflow preventer installation is performed by certified cross connection control and backflow prevention experts in a timely manner conforming to backflow preventer installation and maintenance protocols required by state water board regulations and backflow prevention certification bodies.

sections.heading: Backflow Preventer Repair Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: What is a backflow preventer, and why is it important?
items.answer: A backflow preventer is a device that prevents contaminated water from flowing backward into the potable water supply. It is essential for protecting drinking water from pollutants and health hazards. ‍

items.question: How do I know if my backflow preventer needs repair?
items.answer: Signs that your backflow preventer may require repair include: - Water pressure fluctuations - Leaks around the backflow preventer device - Rust or corrosion on the unit - Unusual noises (such as hissing or vibrating) - Failure to pass a required backflow test ‍

items.question: What are common causes of backflow preventer failure?
items.answer: - Worn-out seals, springs, or rubber components - Mineral buildup or debris clogging the valves - Freezing temperatures causing cracks or damage - High water pressure leading to mechanical failure ‍

items.question: Can my backflow preventer be repaired, or do I need a replacement?
items.answer: Minor issues, such as worn-out seals or clogged check valves, can usually be repaired. However, if the backflow preventer device has significant damage (e.g., cracks, severe corrosion, or a failing internal assembly), replacement may be necessary. ‍

items.question: How long does a backflow preventer repair take?
items.answer: Most repairs take between 60 minutes and a few hours, depending on the severity of the issue and the type of backflow preventer device. ‍

items.question: How much does it cost to repair a backflow preventer?
items.answer: Costs vary depending on the size, model, type of the backflow preventer device, and extent of repairs needed. ‍

items.question: Can I repair my backflow preventer myself?
items.answer: It is not recommended to repair the backflow preventer device yourself. Repairing a backflow preventer requires specialized tools, parts, knowledge of plumbing codes, and a certified test to ensure proper functionality. ‍

items.question: Do I need a permit for backflow preventer repair or replacement?
items.answer: In many areas, backflow preventers are regulated by local water authorities, and a permit may be required for replacement. Check with your water district or municipality for specific requirements. ‍

items.question: How can I prevent my backflow preventer from failing?
items.answer: - Schedule annual testing and maintenance - Keep the area around the preventer clean and debris-free ‍ - Protect outdoor units from freezing temperatures using insulation covers - Ensure proper installation and pressure regulation to prevent stress on the backflow preventer device ‍

items.question: What should I do if my backflow preventer is leaking?
items.answer: If you notice a leak, turn off the water supply to the backflow prevention device and contact a certified backflow tester immediately. Leaks can indicate seal failure, internal damage, or excessive water pressure. ‍

items.question: Who is responsible for maintaining my backflow preventer?
items.answer: Property owners are typically responsible for maintaining, repairing, and testing their backflow preventers to comply with local water safety regulations. ‍

sections.heading: AWWA Certified Backflow Testers Cross Connection Control Specialists
sections.body: Under the regulatory guidelines of the Cross Connection Control Policy Handbook (CCCPH) which replaces Title 17 of the California Code of Regulations, the American Water Works Association (AWWA) is the only agency recognized under new laws for backflow testing certification. Backflow Test Pros is AWWA Certified Backflow Tester and Approved Cross-Connect Specialist + Certified Backflow Testers with County Health Departments across Southern California. As state licensed contractors, and AWWA cross connection specialists, our team of experienced backflow experts work with city water departments throughout Southern California to protect our water, and prevent backflow contamination.

At Backfrlow Test Pros, our certified cross connection control and backflow prevention professionals are trained to perform repairs in a way that ensures the backflow preventer device functions as intended. By properly isolating the preventer device, shutting off the water supply, and carrying out repairs or replacements, our certified technicians reduce your risk of non-compliance and resulting liability.

faqItems.question: What is a backflow preventer, and why is it important?
faqItems.answer: A backflow preventer is a device that prevents contaminated water from flowing backward into the potable water supply. It is essential for protecting drinking water from pollutants and health hazards. ‍

faqItems.question: How do I know if my backflow preventer needs repair?
faqItems.answer: Signs that your backflow preventer may require repair include: - Water pressure fluctuations - Leaks around the backflow preventer device - Rust or corrosion on the unit - Unusual noises (such as hissing or vibrating) - Failure to pass a required backflow test ‍

faqItems.question: What are common causes of backflow preventer failure?
faqItems.answer: - Worn-out seals, springs, or rubber components - Mineral buildup or debris clogging the valves - Freezing temperatures causing cracks or damage - High water pressure leading to mechanical failure ‍

faqItems.question: Can my backflow preventer be repaired, or do I need a replacement?
faqItems.answer: Minor issues, such as worn-out seals or clogged check valves, can usually be repaired. However, if the backflow preventer device has significant damage (e.g., cracks, severe corrosion, or a failing internal assembly), replacement may be necessary. ‍

faqItems.question: How long does a backflow preventer repair take?
faqItems.answer: Most repairs take between 60 minutes and a few hours, depending on the severity of the issue and the type of backflow preventer device. ‍

faqItems.question: How much does it cost to repair a backflow preventer?
faqItems.answer: Costs vary depending on the size, model, type of the backflow preventer device, and extent of repairs needed. ‍

faqItems.question: Can I repair my backflow preventer myself?
faqItems.answer: It is not recommended to repair the backflow preventer device yourself. Repairing a backflow preventer requires specialized tools, parts, knowledge of plumbing codes, and a certified test to ensure proper functionality. ‍

faqItems.question: Do I need a permit for backflow preventer repair or replacement?
faqItems.answer: In many areas, backflow preventers are regulated by local water authorities, and a permit may be required for replacement. Check with your water district or municipality for specific requirements. ‍

faqItems.question: How can I prevent my backflow preventer from failing?
faqItems.answer: - Schedule annual testing and maintenance - Keep the area around the preventer clean and debris-free ‍ - Protect outdoor units from freezing temperatures using insulation covers - Ensure proper installation and pressure regulation to prevent stress on the backflow preventer device ‍

faqItems.question: What should I do if my backflow preventer is leaking?
faqItems.answer: If you notice a leak, turn off the water supply to the backflow prevention device and contact a certified backflow tester immediately. Leaks can indicate seal failure, internal damage, or excessive water pressure. ‍

faqItems.question: Who is responsible for maintaining my backflow preventer?
faqItems.answer: Property owners are typically responsible for maintaining, repairing, and testing their backflow preventers to comply with local water safety regulations. ‍

sections.heading: Backflow Preventer Repair & Replacement Overview
sections.body:
When backflow preventers are faulty, failing, or damaged, it’s important to address these issues promptly to avoid disruption of your water supply. Regular maintenance and testing by qualified our professionals can help identify and fix these common backflow repair issues before they lead to serious problems.

As AWWA Certified backflow testers, cross connection control experts and commercial backflow specialists, we provide leading Southern California commercial clients managed turn-key backflow installation, testing and repair services to ensure compliance and and avoid costly repairs, fines and civil liabilities.

For more detailed review of backflow repair and replacement issues please check our
Backflow Repair & Replacement Frequently Asked Questions

tabs.label: Backflow Repair - Worn or Damaged Seals and Check Valves
tabs.title: Backflow Repair - Worn or Damaged Seals and Check Valves
tabs.body:
All approved backflow preventer assemblies have check valves and seals that prevent water from flowing in the wrong direction. Over time, these components can wear out or become damaged due to age, pressure changes, debris buildup, etc.

Common issues include:

- Worn seals: This can cause leaks around the valve, allowing water to pass through when it shouldn't, leading to potential contamination.
- Damaged check valves: If the check valve is cracked or malfunctioning, water can flow in reverse, bypassing the backflow prevention mechanism.
- Damaged check valve seats: The seat where the check valve aligns to seal off potential backflow is integral to the backflow preventer's integrity. In most cases these check valve seats are replaceable.

links.label: Ask For Backflow Maintenance Coverage!

tabs.label: Backflow Repair - Debris Blockages
tabs.title: Backflow Repair - Debris Blockages
tabs.body: Debris, rust, or sediment can accumulate in your backflow preventer. This can prevent the backflow prevention device from functioning correctly, leading to: - Leaking Backflow: If the device is an Reduced Pressure Principle type of backflow, which is very common in California, debris in the line will most likely cause it to visibly leak. - Failing Backflow: Even the smallest amount of debris in the backflow preventer can cause the results for its annual testing to be below acceptable values.- Faulty Backflow: If debris clogs the valve or chamber, it could prevent the device from opening or closing properly, making it unable to stop backflow. - Restricted flow: Blockages can restrict normal water flow, leading to pressure issues in the system and potentially damaging the device over time. ‍

links.label: Ask For Backflow Maintenance Coverage!

tabs.label: Backflow Repair - Faulty Relief Valve
tabs.title: Backflow Repair - Faulty Relief Valve
tabs.body: The relief valve is a critical component to the reduced pressure principle backflow preventer (RPP). In the event that the check valves fail, the relief valve will always dump any backflow to the atmosphere through the relief valve vent. This is why the RPP is the preferred choice whenever a potential contaminant is connected to the potable water supply. If the relief valve is damaged or malfunctioning, it can cause: - Leaking Relief Valve: A common symptom of relief valve damage is leaking from the relief valve vent. This vent is directly underneath the backflow. Leaking vents waste water, and can cause serious water damage quickly if left unrepaired. - Failure to Prevent Backflow: A relief valve that is failing to open will not properly prevent backflow from occurring. Usually relief valves stick in the closed position due to scale or hard water build up. - Pulsating Relief Valve: Sometimes relief valves can pulsate, which is usually due to air being trapped inside the relief valve. This should be resolved immediately as the pulsating can get more and more intense causing plumbing damage. ‍

links.label: Ask For Backflow Maintenance Coverage!

tabs.label: Backflow Repair - Corrosion and Scale Buildup
tabs.title: Backflow Repair - Corrosion and Scale Buildup
tabs.body: Backflow preventer devices are often exposed to harsh conditions, including water with high mineral content, chlorine, or other problematic elements. Over time, this can lead to: - Corrosion of internal components: Corroded parts, such as metal components like springs, seats, and valves, may fail to operate as intended. In addition, the internal rubber/plastic parts wear down over time. - Mineral deposits (Scale Build Up): Hard water deposits, and other minerals can build up inside the backflow device, causing improper opening/closing of valves. This is the most common cause of relief valves on Reduced Pressure Principle Backflow Preventers not opening properly. - Rust Accumulation: Rust can collect inside the shut off valves or the body of the backflow eventually washing into the critical areas of the valve. These pieces of rust can obstruct the backflow preventer from working properly, and cause leaks. ‍

links.label: Ask For Backflow Maintenance Coverage!

tabs.label: Backflow Repair - Vandalized or Damaged Body
tabs.title: Backflow Repair - Vandalized or Damaged Body
tabs.body: The main body of the backflow preventer is responsible for housing all the vital internal and external components. These components are integral for backflow prevention, and annual testing. Vandalism, corrosion, warping, or fractures in the body can lead to: - Leaks: Damage to the backflow body may cause the backflow preventer to leak. These leaks may result in wasted water and property damage. In some cases emergency water shut down may be necessary. - Annual Testing Failure: Internal components depend on the integrity of the valve body to achieve acceptable backflow testing results. On the other hand, external components connected to the body are relied on by the certified backflow tester to confirm the backflow preventer is function properly. - Repair Failure: Damage or warping of the backflow preventer body may result in repair attempts being unsuccessful. In such cases, the entire backflow preventer will need to be replaced. Installation will require an approved backflow preventer by your local water authority. ‍

links.label: Ask For Backflow Maintenance Coverage!

tabs.label: Backflow Repair - Improper Installation
tabs.title: Backflow Repair - Improper Installation
tabs.body: If your backflow preventer isn't installed correctly, it can lead to various ongoing issues, such as: - Inadequate Backflow Prevention: Backflow preventers need to be installed according to code to ensure they are functioning as per their original design. Any installation deviation may lead to failure to prevent backflow. - Water Supply Disruption: As soon as an improper installation is identified it must be corrected immediately. Failure to do so will lead to water service termination by the water department. ‍

links.label: Ask For Backflow Maintenance Coverage!

tabs.label: Backflow Repair - Worn or Broken Valve Springs
tabs.title: Backflow Repair - Worn or Broken Valve Springs
tabs.body: Most backflow preventers rely on springs to open and close valves in response to pressure changes.If the springs become worn, damaged, or corroded, they can: - Fail to open/close properly: A weak or damaged spring might not allow the valve to open or close fully, preventing the device from effectively stopping backflow. This can also lead to leaks at the backflow preventer. - Stick in one position: If the spring or valve mechanism becomes stuck, it can leave the device in either the open or closed position. This can cause the backflow preventer to leak and no longer properly protect the water supply. ‍

links.label: Ask For Backflow Maintenance Coverage!

tabs.label: Backflow Repair - Failure of Shut Off or Isolation Valves
tabs.title: Backflow Repair - Failure of Shut Off or Isolation Valves
tabs.body: Shut off or isolation valves are used during testing and maintenance to isolate the backflow preventer from the rest of the plumbing system. f these components fail, it can lead to: - Inability to repair the backflow preventer: Failing shut off valves make repairs to the backflow preventer more difficult. In some cases, shut off valves will need to be replaced before any repairs can be made to the device. - Inability to test the backflow preventer: If shut off valves don’t close properly, the certified backflow tester will be unable to test the device. These results in a fail, and the shut off valve will need to be replaced before retesting. Usually we recommend complete backflow preventer replacement in such cases. ‍

links.label: Ask For Backflow Maintenance Coverage!

tabs.label: Backflow Repair - Wear & Tear on Internal Parts
tabs.title: Backflow Repair - Wear & Tear on Internal Parts
tabs.body: Backflow preventers often include moving parts such as springs, rubber parts, and plastic parts that all combined to guarantee water safety.Over time, wear and tear can cause these components to fail, resulting in: - Leaking Backflow Preventer: Internal parts no longer sealing properly can cause the device to leak. Repairs should be made at first signs of leak. - Annual Test Failure: Periodically, these parts do wear out, which is one of the main reasons for your annual testing. Once we identify the issue, we can replace, and rebuild almost any internal components to the backflow preventer. ‍

links.label: Ask For Backflow Maintenance Coverage!

tabs.label: Backflow Repair - Pressure Fluctuations or Water Hammer
tabs.title: Backflow Repair - Pressure Fluctuations or Water Hammer
tabs.body: Sudden pressure changes, or "water hammer," can damage a backflow device.Water hammer occurs when the flow of water is abruptly stopped or changed, causing a shockwave through the pipes. This can lead to: - Damaged components: The shockwave can damage internal components, such as check valves, leading to a leaking and failing backflow preventer. - Leaks and fractures: Excessive water hammer can cause cracks or leaks in the body or valves of the backflow preventer. - Relief Valve Spitting & Wasted Water: Excessive pressure fluctuations on a line with a Reduced Pressure Principle Backflow Preventer will result in constant spitting of the relief valve. Our recommended solution to address both water hammer & pressure fluctuations is the installation of a soft seated check valve on either side of the backflow preventer. ‍

links.label: Ask For Backflow Maintenance Coverage!

sections.heading: Backflow Test Pros Backflow Repair Service Areas
sections.body: Because backflow preventer devices are critical to maintaining clean, safe water supplies, California State Water Districts, County Health Boards and Local Municipalities regulations mandate that only licensed and certified backflow technicians and plumbers who have the expertise, training, have the legal authority to perform repairs or replacements in compliance with regulations and with the necessary quality control to protect both your business and the public water system. At Backfrlow Test Pros, our certified cross connection control and backflow prevention professionals are trained to perform repairs in a way that ensures the backflow preventer device functions as intended. By properly isolating the preventer device, shutting off the water supply, and carrying out repairs or replacements, our certified technicians reduce your risk of non-compliance and resulting liability.
